coredata.dc:title: N-doped carbon dots sensor for selective detection of hydroxylamine hydrochloride
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Jiangyao
Dong, Yan
Yang, Xueyun
Yao, Cheng
coredata.dc:description: The preparation of N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) was facile and efficient by the hydrothermal method, using diethylenetriamine (DETA) and citric acid (CA) as nitrogen source and carbon source, respectively. The fluorescence of N-CDs was effectively quenched by Fe3+. Upon addition of hydroxylamine hydrochloride (HH) to N-CDs/Fe3+ complex dispersion, the fluorescence of N-CDs was significantly recovered. Therefore, we developed an “off-on” fluorescence assay for the detection of HH using N-CDs/Fe3+ as a fluorescence probe. The fluorescence intensity showed linearity with the added HH concentration ranging from 0 to 100 μM with the detection limit of 0.246 μM (S/N = 3). The proposed N-CDs sensing showed high selectivity and sensitivity to detection HH in aqueous solution.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped carbon dots
Fluorescence probe
Hydroxylamine hydrochloride
Sensitivity

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Specialized Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Higher Education of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image, (b) size distribution histogram of N-CDs.
(a) UV-vis absorption, excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs. (b) Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs at different excitation wavelengths. N-CDs: 0.025 g/L.
FT-IR spectrum of the synthesized N-CDs.
XPS spectra of N-CDs (a), C1s (b), O1s (c)and N1s (d).
Fluorescence intensities of N-CDs (a) in different concentration solutions of NaCl, (b) in different UV irradiation time, (c) in nine months.

(a) Effect of pH on N-CDs/Fe3+/HH system. (b) The fluorescence intensity difference values of N-CDs in different concentration solutions of Fe3+. (c) Reaction time of N-CDs/Fe3+/HH. (F0 and F1 were fluorescence intensities of N-CDs at 435 nm in the absence and presence of Fe3+).

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs with gradual increases of Fe3+ concentration (0–500 μM) at 360 nm excitation. (b) The linear relationship plot between normalized intensity (F0–F1) and Fe3+ with a concentration range 0–90 μM. (F0 and F1 were fluorescence intensities of N-CDs at 435 nm in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively).

The fluorescence intensity difference values of the as-prepared N-CDs/Fe3+ to HH and various interfering agent. (F1 and F2 were fluorescence intensities of N-CDs/Fe3+ at 447 nm in the absence and presence of HH, respectively).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs with gradual increases of HH concentration (0–700 μM) at 360 nm excitation. (b) The dependence of fluorescence intensity difference values on the concentration of HH. (c) The linear relationship plot between normalized intensity (F2–F1) and HH concentration (0–100 μM). (F1 and F2 were fluorescence intensities of N-CDs/Fe3+ at 447 nm in the absence and presence of HH, respectively).
Time dependent ﬂuorescence decay spectra with an excitation at 360 nm.
Schematic of the fabrication and sensing process of Fe3+ and HH.
Energy levels in the N-CDs with Fe3+ and photoelectron transfer mechanism.
Comparison of the presented method with other approaches for HH detection.
Analytical results (n = 3) of HH in real samples.

head.ce:title: N-doped carbon dots sensor for selective detection of hydroxylamine hydrochloride

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing, 211816, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing, 211816, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The preparation of N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) was facile and efficient by the hydrothermal method, using diethylenetriamine (DETA) and citric acid (CA) as nitrogen source and carbon source, respectively. The fluorescence of N-CDs was effectively quenched by Fe3+. Upon addition of hydroxylamine hydrochloride (HH) to N-CDs/Fe3+ complex dispersion, the fluorescence of N-CDs was significantly recovered. Therefore, we developed an “off-on” fluorescence assay for the detection of HH using N-CDs/Fe3+ as a fluorescence probe. The fluorescence intensity showed linearity with the added HH concentration ranging from 0 to 100 μM with the detection limit of 0.246 μM (S/N = 3). The proposed N-CDs sensing showed high selectivity and sensitivity to detection HH in aqueous solution.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydroxylamine hydrochloride (HH, also called as hydroxylammonium chloride or oxammonium hydrochloride) has been applied in many fields, such as a reductant for the synthesis of anticancer drugs (Hydroxyurea), sulfa drugs (Sulfamethoxazole), and pesticides (Methomyl), a raw material for the synthesis of oxime and an inorganic catalyst of transamidation [1]. However, excessive HH is genotoxic and mutagenic to a variety of organisms. So, the detection of HH plays crucial roles for ensuring the quality of drugs and pesticides and reducing the harm to organisms. In the past few years, many analytical methods have been proposed for the determination of HH, including copper phosphomolybdate spectrophotometry, resonance light scattering, kinetic spectrophotometry, and ion chromatography, etc. However, the methods above require costly instruments and complicated procedures and restrain HH detection in practical applications. Compared to traditional techniques, fluorescence sensing has become a valid method in analytical fields due to its facilely, economically, high sensitivity and stable performances [2–9]. To our knowledge, there is rarely reported study for HH sensing through N-CDs so far.

Over the past decade, carbon dots (CDs, also known as carbon quantum dots or carbon nanoparticles) are one kind of newly discovered fluorescent nanomaterials that have become increasingly popular as a result of their exclusive optical properties, easy synthesis, great aqueous stability, good biocompatibility, low toxicity [10]. They are considered attractive nanomaterials with vast potential applications in various fields, including fluorescent sensor, photocatalysis, bioimaging, light-emitting diodes, solar cells, photodynamic therapy and drug delivery [11–13]. Carbon dots have unique optical properties, and the presence of surface functional groups enable carbon dots to obtain multi-purpose nanomaterials. Interactions between functional groups and small molecules or ions change the optical properties and enable them to be used in sensing applications [14]. The size and surface functional groups of carbon dots should be modulated for possessing optical properties. In particular, the emission of CDs could be adjusted by changing the condensation reaction or most common doping of other elements [15–18]. Several strategies for improving the fluorescence emission of CDs have been reported. One of the most famous methods is doping N and S between the carbon source and the compounds containing these atoms with the hydrothermal method. Among these doping atoms, the N-doping seems to be the most common method that can advance some performance of CDs including providing more active sites and large surface area, reducing the surface energy and enhancing catalytic activity [19]. In addition, it has been proved that doping CDs with nitrogen atoms is a feasible method to improve the quantum yields (QYs) of CDs because the nitrogen-doping process can regulate surface defect of CDs [20–23].

In this paper, there is an easy way to synthesize N-CDs through the pyrolysis channel using citric acid monohydrate (CA) and diethylenetriamine (DETA). The synthesized N-CDs present high quantum yield of up to 71% using quinine sulfate as a reference. Then we investigated a fluorescent turn-off-on method using the as-prepared N-CDs as fluorescent probes to obtain a facile, sensitive and selective analysis of HH. When Fe3+ was added to the N-CDs solution, the fluorescence of N-CDs was significantly quenched due to non-radiative electron transfer. The fluorescence of N-CDs/Fe3+ was restored excellently by the addition of HH because of the reduction reaction between HH and Fe3+.

Citric acid monohydrate (CA), diethylenetriamine (DETA), quinine sulfate, sulfuric acid, sodium chloride (NaCl), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), Tris-HCl buffer solution, ascorbic acid (AA), hydrochloric acid solutions and HH were acquired from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Ferric trichloride (FeCl3) was purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation. Standard stock solution of Fe3+ (1.0 mol L−1) was prepared by dissolving FeCl3 in Tris-HCl (pH 3). Work solutions of Fe3+ were obtained by diluting standard stock solutions. Standard stock solution of HH (1.0 mol L−1) was prepared by dissolving HH in deionized water. All reagents were analytical grade and used without further purification. Na2S2O3, NaBr, NaI, NaNO3, NaH2PO4, NaNO2, Na2SO3, NaHSO3, NaSCN, NaHCO3, Na2CO3 and H2C2O4 were used. Deionized (DI) water (18.2 MΩ cm) from a Millipore Auto-pure WR600A system (Millipore Ltd, USA) was used in all experiments. Other reagents were purchased from Shanghai Ling Feng Chemical Co., Ltd.

Fluorescence spectra were purchased by using a FS5-SC-05 fluorescence spectrometer. UV-Vis absorption spectra were recorded on a pre double beam UV-Vis spectrophotometer with a 10 mm optical path length quartz cuvette. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were carried out on a FEI TECNAI F20 EM with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained on a Thermo Nicolet 360 FT-IR spectrophotometer with a wave number range of 4000–500 cm−1. X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were collected with an AXIS ULTRA DLD with an Al Kα X-ray monochromator and a pass energy of 40 eV. Electrochemical measurements are made at CHI 832D electrochemical workstation including a glassy carbon electrode (GCE, d = 3 mm), an Ag/AgCl electrode and a platinum wire as the working, reference and counter electrode, respectively. The pH adjustments were performed by pH-3C.

According to the literature with some modifications, N-CDs were synthesized through hydrothermal method [24,25]. In brief, 0.28 g of CA and 0.56 g of DETA were dissolved in 100 mL of deionized water. The solution was heated 170 °C for 6 h in a polytetrafluoroethylene autoclaves (50 mL) and then the solution became brown-yellow. After naturally cooling to room temperature, the as-prepared N-CDs was collected centrifuged at a high speed (15000 r/min) for 15 min to transfer large particles. The solution was dialyzed with dialysis membrane (MWCO = 3.5 kDa) for 12 h to remove the unreacted ions and fine particles. N-CDs were reserved in refrigerator at 4 °C for later use.

We determined the quantum yield of N-CDs by using quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (QY = 54%) as the standard sample. Then N-CDs were diluted in deionized water to seven different concentrations for measuring the absorbance and the fluorescence intensity. In order to minimize the re-absorption effects, the absorption values of individual solution should be kept below 0.1 at the excitation wavelength [26]. The quantum yield was calculated according to the following equation:
QY =
QY
s
×
(
F
u
/
F
s
)
×
(
A
s
/
A
u
)
×
(
η
u
/
η
s
)
2
where “QY” is the quantum yield, “F” is the measured integrated emission intensity, “A” represents the absorbance, “η” is the refractive index of the solution. The subscript “s” refers to the quinine sulfate with known QY and “u” for sample.

In a classic assay, 2 mL solution of N-CDs (0.025 g L−1) was added in a centrifuge tube (5 mL), followed by the addition of a series of concentrations of Fe3+ (concentration range 0–500 μM) and the fluorescence was quenched subsequently.

Then, HH solutions with different concentrations (from 0 to 700 μM) were obtained by dissolving standard stock solution of HH (1.0 mol L−1) in deionized water. The selectivity of HH was investigated by the addition of 100 μL HH solution (500 μM) to compare other reductants or anions, which was made up of 2 mL N-CDs solution (0.025 g L−1) and 100 μL Fe3+ solution (320 μM). The fluorescence was recovered at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. All experiments were performed at the room temperature and parallel three times.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of synthesized N-CDs was represented in Fig. 1 a. TEM images could be clearly seen that the synthesized N-CDs were nearly spherical in shape [27]. N-CDs appeared relatively uniform in size in Fig. 1b. The average diameter of N-CDs was 5.9 nm. Moreover, a uniform dispersion without obvious aggregation was descried.

The UV-vis absorption and photoluminescent spectra of N-CDs were showed in Fig. 2 a. UV-Vis absorption spectrum of N-CDs was depicted by black color. The absorption band at 210 nm could be ascribed to π-π* of the CC bond. The inferior peak at 315 nm reflects n-π* transition of CO on the surface of N-CDs. The strong fluorescence of N-CDs might be caused by the surface traps of the nitrogen-doped. As shown in Fig. 2a, a strong emission could be observed under excitation at 360 nm with the maximum emission wavelength at 435 nm. The emission peaks of N-CDs almost did not shift when excitation wavelengths were changed, and the maximum emission peak at different excitation wavelengths always maintained at 435 nm (Fig. 2b). The properties of excitation-independent emissions were greatly different from most of the previous reported N-CDs, which could successfully avoid auto fluorescence during their applications [28]. The results of fluorescence spectra further confirmed the size and surface uniformity of N-CDs.

The FT-IR analysis was used to investigate the surface functional groups of N-CDs, as shown in Fig. 3 . The FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs displayed some bands due to the oxygen-containing groups. For example, the broad band centered at 3420 cm−1 was derived from the stretching mode of C–OH, the band at 1295 and 1261 cm−1 were belonged to C–O stretching vibrations [29]. The band at 1589 cm−1 resulted from the stretching mode of COO−; 1118 and 1072 cm−1 were attributed to C–H bending vibrations. 1397 and 3115 cm−1 were corresponding to asymmetric stretching vibrations of C–N and N–H, which were attributed to DETA [30]. These functional groups improved the hydrophilicity and stability of N-CDs, which made the enormous potential of the material for sensing in aqueous samples [31].

The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 4 a) displayed three typical peaks of C1s at 284.9 eV, O1s at 530.9 eV and N1s at 400.0 eV, with relative atomic percentages of 67.97%, 14.99% and 17.04%, respectively [32]. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 4b) was split into three peaks at 284.94, 286.10 and 287.82 eV, which were ascribed to C–C, C–N, and C= O/O–CO. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 4c) exhibited two peaks at 530.89 and 531.76 eV arising from CO and C–OH/C–O–C. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 4d) could be divided into three components corresponding to pyridinic N at 399.12 eV, pyrrolic N at 400.02 eV and graphitic N at 401.32 eV. These results were consistent with those illustrated in FT-IR spectra. All the conclusions from the FT-IR spectroscopy and XPS study confirmed that the surface of the CDs was functionalized by oxygen and nitrogen groups, and most hydrophilic groups on the surface led to the stable existence of N-CDs in aqueous solutions. The N-CDs were intrinsically water-soluble due to the oxygen-rich functional groups in the structures. The fluorescence stability of N-CDs was evaluated by measuring the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs under extreme high ionic strengths, long time illumination and high temperature. First, the fluorescence intensity decreased less than 5% with the increase of NaCl concentration ranging from 0 to 1.0 mol L−1 (Fig. 5 a), which indicated the great stability in high-salinity and provided the essential situation for N-CDs to be used in the presence of physical salt concentrations. Second, as could be seen in Fig. 5b, the optical stability of N-CDs was detected for 120 min after UV irradiation, the fluorescence signal of N-CDs remained stable within the studied time, illustrating a distinguished photostability. Third, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs remained almost unchanged even after 6 months in air at room temperature, showing good stability (Fig. 5c). Forth, the fluorescence intensity decreased as temperature increased, but when N-CDs solution was returned to room temperature, the fluorescence was recovered, exhibiting remarkable stability in room temperature environment [33–36].

The quantum yields of the synthesized N-CDs were measured by a relative method with quinine sulfate as reference. Quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 mol L−1 H2SO4 (literature quantum yield of 54% at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm) was chosen as the reference [37]. Results showed that the quantum yield of N-CDs was 71%.

The experimental conditions were optimized prior to HH assay. The effects of pH on sensitivity were investigated. When the Tris-HCl buffer solution with pH 3.0 was used, F2–F1 (F1 and F2 are fluorescence intensities at 447 nm in the absence and presence of HH) reached highest (Fig. 6 a), and Fe3+ did not hydrolyze. Moreover, Fe3+ would precipitate in the form of Fe(OH)3 while pH > 4. Hence, pH 3.0 Tris-HCl buffer solution was elected in this work. With the increase of N-CDs concentration, self-quenching could occur since the collision frequency will be high. Therefore, the high concentration of N-CDs leads to decrease fluorescence intensity. On this account, concentration of N-CDs was determined to be 0.025 g L−1 for the further HH sensing. Then, it was speculated that the concentration of Fe3+ played a significant part in the investigated system due to Fe3+ reducing the fluorescence of N-CDs. Thus, the concentration of Fe3+ in the range 0–500 μM, as shown in Fig. 6b. It was found that Fe3+ solution at a concentration of 320 μM was the most suitable. The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs/Fe3+/HH increased with the increase of time, it remained steady when reaction time was 2 h (Fig. 6c) at room temperature.

The presence of abundant functional groups in N-CDs prompted us to further investigate their potential sensing applications. Through extensively screening, N-CDs was found to exhibited an interesting “OFF-ON” two-state emission with the stepwise addition of Fe3+ and HH. As shown in a proposed sensing process (Scheme 1 ), the observed fluorescence quenching by Fe3+ could be attributed to non-radiative electron transfer from the excited state of N-CDs to the d orbital of Fe3+ [38]. With further addition of HH, Fe3+ was stripped from N-CDs and the emission of N-CDs was recovered. These findings allowed N-CDs to behave a potentially dual-function sensing platform for recognizing Fe3+ and HH.

We researched the feasibility of applying such N-CDs to Fe3+, whereas no obvious signal change was observed for other substances. The fluorescence signal of N-CDs was quickly quenched (2min) when Fe3+ was added to N-CDs solution. Upon the addition of Fe3+, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased at 435 nm, indicating that the fluorescence intensity of the mixture was sensitive to concentration of Fe3+ (Fig. 7 a). The quenching (F0–F1) showed a good linear relationship versus the concentration of Fe3+ in the range of 0–90 μM (Fig. 7b), where F0 and F1 are fluorescence intensities at 435 nm in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively.

In view of the strong oxidation of Fe3+, it was proposed that the ensemble of N-CDs and Fe3+ was a potential sensing platform for HH. Then we explored the response of N-CDs/Fe3+ to HH and the other agents. The same level (2.250 mM) of CO3 2−, I−, Cl−, Br−, NO3 −, NO2 −, H2C2O4, SCN−, S2O3 2− (1.125 mM), SO3 2− (1.125 mM), HSO3 −, H2PO4 − (1.125 mM), HCO3 − (0.750 mM) and AA (1.125 mM), was added into N-CDs/Fe3+, respectively, as shown in Fig. 8 . Others showed almost no fluorescence improvement of N-CDs/Fe3+, except HH. The high selectivity of this probe provides a potential for the efficient detection of HH. The fluorescence intensity gradually recovered with the increasing concentration of HH (Fig. 9 a), such result indicated that N-CDs/Fe3+ sensing system was sensitive to HH, and the “off” state of N-CDs quenched by Fe3+ could be turned “on” by HH. The value of F2–F1 increased gradually with increasing HH concentration (Fig. 9b) and a linear response in the range from 0 to 100 μM was obtained (Fig. 9c). The linear regression equation was: y = 11.84x + 64.58, R
2 = 0.998 and the limit of detection was estimated to be 0.246 μM (calculated according to a signal-to-noise ratio of S/N = 3). In spite of the fact that, there are some studies of condign LOD values with this study (Table 1 ), but our method is very facile and precursor is low-cost. These advantages offer this N-CDs-based “off-on” fluorescent probe an excellent platform for the detection of HH. To confirm the high efficiency of the N-CDs sensing system in real samples, the sensor was tested in industrial grade samples. The recoveries were from 94.7% to 102.6% and relative standard deviation (RSD) was all below 5%, confirming that the proposed method could be applied to real samples with high accuracy (Table 2 ).

The ﬂuorescence quenching might be caused by energy transfer, excited state reaction, electron transfer, molecular rearrangement, collision quenching or forming a complex with the ground state of the ﬂuorophore. Run et al. explained that the fluorescence quenching eﬀect because of direct interactions between Fe3+ and –NH2 and –COOH groups of N-CDs [43]. Ying et al. reported the quenching mechanism of simultaneous dynamic and static quenching [44]. Anam et al. deciphered quenching process of static mechanism [45].

When Fe3+ was added to N-CDs, the functional groups (-OH identified by the FT-IR spectrum) of N-CDs occurred the complexation with Fe3+, resulting in the splitting of the d orbital of Fe3+, due to good binding affinity between hydroxyl groups and Fe3+ [46]. Therefore, the partial electrons in the excited state of N-CDs were transferred to the d orbital of Fe3+ and this could be called photoinduced electron transfer (PET). Electron transition (fluorescence emission) in the form of radiation was hindered, leading to the fluorescence quenching. To further know the quenching mechanism, the energy levels of N-CDs should be researched. The HOMO values of the N-CDs were calculated from cyclic voltammogram (Fig. S1) and it was found to be:
E
H O M O
= −
E
O X
− 4.70 e V
where
E
O X
is the oxidation peak potential of N-CDs in the cyclic voltammogram relative to the normal hydrogen electrode.
E
H O M O
= − 0.29 e V − 4.70
e V = − 4.99
e V
E
L U M O
=
E
g
− 4.99 e V = 3.06 e V − 4.99 e V = − 1.93 e V

Fig. S2 displayed that the Tauc plot extracted from absorbance measurement. E
g was 3.06 eV. The quenching of N-CDs was due to the transfer of electrons from the excited state of the N-CDs (−1.93eV) to the LUMO orbitals of Fe3+ (−2.40 eV) [47], as shown in Scheme 2 .

The zeta potential values of N-CDs in the presence and absence of Fe3+ were investigated for confirming quenching mechanism of N-CDs. The average zeta potential value of N-CDs was −7.37 mV. The negative potential might be the dissociation of –COOH groups on N-CDs surface. In addition, when Fe3+ was added to N-CDs, the zeta potential inversely backed to the positive value of +23.6 mV. Since Fe3+ complexed with the surface functional groups of N-CDs, the positive charge on the surface of N-CDs/Fe3+ could be recorded. This consequence was consistent with other confirmation that Fe3+ was correlated on the surface of N-CDs. With the addition of HH, the average zeta potential of the system was −4.98 mV, which is the redox reaction between HH and Fe3+, indicating subdued interaction between Fe3+ and N-CDs. Then, the time-resolved fluorescence decay of N-CDs in the presence and absence of Fe3+ were studied. As shown in Fig. 10 , the fluorescence lifetimes of N-CDs before and after adding Fe3+ were 14.76 ns and 12.25 ns, respectively. This consequence revealed that the quenching mechanism of N-CDs by Fe3+ was dynamic quenching and it was more indicative of PET.

In view of reduction reaction between Fe3+ and HH, N-CDs/Fe3+ could serve as a “turn-on” fluorescent sensor for the detection of HH. For N-CDs/Fe3+, Fe3+ could be reduced to Fe2+ by HH, resulting in weakening of PET and recovery of N-CDs fluorescence. The reaction equation of HH and Fe3+ was as follows:
2Fe3+/N-CDs + 2NH2OH·HCl = 2Fe2+ + N2 ↑ + 4H+ + 2H2O + 2Cl- + N-CDs

We added 1, 10-phenanthroline to the solutions of Fe3+/N-CDs (Fig. S3, curve a) and Fe3+/HH/N-CDs (Fig. S3, curve b) and observed the UV-vis absorption spectra for verifying the formation of Fe2+. There was an obvious absorption band at around 510 nm in spectral b, which could be attributed to the complex of Fe2+ and 1, 10-phenanthroline. In addition, the orange complexes of Fe2+ and 1, 10-phenanthroline could be observed under visible light (Fig. S3).

In summary, we researched a simple, moderate hydrothermal method using CA and DETA as materials to prepare high quantum yield N-CDs for the detection of HH. N-CDs exhibited great photostability and excitation-independent emission behavior. The fluorescent probe based on N-CDs/Fe3+ system for detection HH because of the reduction reaction between HH and Fe3+. The feasibility of this probe was successfully verified by detecting HH in real samples. A good linear relationship was obtained from 0 to 100 μM, with a detection limit of 0.246 μM. Compared to those conventional analytical techniques, fluorescence sensing was simple, high sensitivity, selectivity, linear range wide and stable in practical applications.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the Specialized Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Higher Education (20123221110012). The authors also thank our teachers and other schoolmate for their participating in this work.

The following are the supplementary data to this article:
APPLICATION APPLICATION

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.optmat.2019.05.035.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Transamidation of primary amides with amines using hydroxylamine hydrochloride as an inorganic catalyst

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.L. Allen, B.N. Atkinson, J.M.J. Williams, Transamidation of primary amides with amines using hydroxylamine hydrochloride as an inorganic catalyst, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 51 (2012) 1383-1386.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A quinoline-based Cu2+ ion complex fluorescence probe for selective detection of inorganic phosphate anion in aqueous solution and its application to living cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Dai, P. Wang, J. Fu, K. Yao, K. Xu, X. Pang, A quinoline-based Cu2+ ion complex fluorescence probe for selective detection of inorganic phosphate anion in aqueous solution and its application to living cells, Spectrochimica Acta Part A 183 (2017) 30-36.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rapid microwave assisted synthesis of molecularly imprinted polymers on carbon quantum dots for fluorescent sensing of tetracycline in milk

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Hou, H. Li, L. Wang, P. Zhang, T. Zhou, H. Ding, L. Ding, Rapid microwave assisted synthesis of molecularly imprinted polymers on carbon quantum dots for fluorescent sensing of tetracycline in milk, Talanta 146 (2016) 34-40.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly sensitive label-free electrochemiluminescence aptasensor for early detection of myoglobin, a biomarker for myocardial infarction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.R.K. Pur, M. Hosseini, F. Faridbod, M.R. Ganjali, Highly sensitive label-free electrochemiluminescence aptasensor for early detection of myoglobin, a biomarker for myocardial infarction, Microchim. Acta 184 (2017) 3529-3537.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescence based turn-on strategy for determination of microRNA-155 using DNA-templated copper nanoclusters

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.S. Borghei, M. Hosseini, M.R. Ganjali, Fluorescence based turn-on strategy for determination of microRNA-155 using DNA-templated copper nanoclusters, Microchim. Acta 184 (2017) 2671-2677.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A fluorometric aptamer based assay for cytochrome C using fluorescent graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Salehnia, M. Hosseini, M.R. Ganjali, A fluorometric aptamer based assay for cytochrome C using fluorescent graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets, Microchim. Acta 184 (2017) 2157-2163.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: FRET-based aptamer biosensor for selective and sensitive detection of aflatoxin B1 in peanut and rice

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F.S. Sabet, M. Hosseini, H. Khabbaz, M. Dadmehr, M.R. Ganjali, FRET-based aptamer biosensor for selective and sensitive detection of aflatoxin B1 in peanut and rice, Food Chem. 220 (2017) 527-532.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Label-free fluorescent detection of microRNA-155 based on synthesis of hairpin DNA-templated copper nanoclusters by etching (top-down approach)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.S. Borghei, M. Hosseini, M.R. Ganjali, S. Hosseinkhani, Label-free fluorescent detection of microRNA-155 based on synthesis of hairpin DNA-templated copper nanoclusters by etching (top-down approach), Sens. Actuators B 248 (2017) 133-139.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual-emission of Eu based metal-organic frameworks hybrids with carbon dots for ratiometric fluorescent detection of Cr (VI)

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent carbon dots and their sensing applications
